content.text: Cloud Shell is a managed container that Azure provisions for you. You do not install anything to get started: the container ships with a broad set of tools covering Azure management, Kubernetes, infrastructure-as-code, scripting languages, text processing, source control, and more. This means you can start working immediately without configuring a local development environment.

The tool set is maintained by Azure and updated regularly. Versions may differ slightly from what you have installed locally, so it is a good practice to check versions before running version-sensitive scripts.

The primary tools for interacting with Azure resources are pre-installed and pre-authenticated in Cloud Shell. You do not need to log in when starting a session; Cloud Shell uses your portal credentials automatically.
Command-line interface for all Azure services
High-performance file transfer tool for Azure Storage
Local dev and deployment for Azure Functions
End-to-end developer toolchain for Azure deployment

# Verify Azure CLI and authenticate (already done via portal)
az version
az account show --query '{subscription:name, user:user.name}' -o table

# Use Bicep to compile and deploy an ARM template
cat > main.bicep << 'EOF'
param location string = resourceGroup().location
param storageAccountName string

resource sa 'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts@2023-01-01' = {
  name: storageAccountName
  location: location
  sku: { name: 'Standard_LRS' }
  kind: 'StorageV2'
}
EOF

bicep build main.bicep      # compiles to main.json
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --parameters storageAccountName=mystg$(date +%s | tail -c 6)

# Verify Az module in PowerShell mode
Get-Module Az -ListAvailable | Select-Object Name, Version | Select-Object -First 5
Get-AzContext

# AzCopy example: copy files to blob storage
$token = (Get-AzAccessToken -ResourceUrl 'https://storage.azure.com/').Token
azcopy copy ~/clouddrive/data.csv \
  'https://mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net/uploads/data.csv' \
  --oauth-token $token
Cloud Shell includes the full Kubernetes CLI toolchain. When you connect to an AKS cluster, you can manage workloads directly without installing anything locally.
Azure AD authentication plugin for kubectl
Container image management (no daemon — use registry commands only)
Scaffold Kubernetes manifests and Helm charts

# Connect to an AKS cluster
az aks get-credentials \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKSCluster

# Verify connection
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces

# Deploy a Helm chart
helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx
helm repo update
helm install ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx \
  --namespace ingress-nginx \
  --create-namespace \
  --set controller.replicaCount=2

# Monitor a deployment rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/ingress-nginx-controller -n ingress-nginx
Cloud Shell does not run a Docker daemon, so you cannot build images with 'docker build'. Use Azure Container Registry Tasks (az acr build) or Azure DevOps pipelines to build and push images instead.

# Build a container image using ACR Tasks (no local Docker daemon needed)
az acr build \
  --registry myRegistry \
  --image myapp:$(git rev-parse --short HEAD) \
  --file Dockerfile .

# Pull and inspect image metadata (no daemon; uses skopeo if available, or az acr)
az acr repository show-tags \
  --name myRegistry \
  --repository myapp \
  --output table
Cloud Shell ships with several programming language runtimes so you can write and run scripts, test utility functions, or use SDK-based tools without installing anything.
Azure Functions (Node), JavaScript tooling, npm packages
C# scripting, .NET Azure Functions, test runners

# Python 3: use the Azure SDK
pip install --user azure-mgmt-compute --quiet

python3 << 'EOF'
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient
import os

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
sub_id = os.environ['AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID']
client = ComputeManagementClient(credential, sub_id)

vms = list(client.virtual_machines.list_all())
for vm in vms[:5]:
    print(f'{vm.name} ({vm.location})')
EOF

# Node.js: run a quick script
node -e "
const { execSync } = require('child_process');
const subs = JSON.parse(execSync('az account list').toString());
subs.forEach(s => console.log(s.name, s.id));
"

# Go: build a small tool
mkdir -p ~/clouddrive/tools/hello && cd ~/clouddrive/tools/hello
cat > main.go << 'EOF'
package main
import "fmt"
func main() { fmt.Println("Hello from Cloud Shell Go") }
EOF
go run main.go
Cloud Shell includes Terraform, Ansible, and Bicep out of the box, making it a convenient environment for IaC operations against Azure without configuring a local toolchain.

# Ansible: run an Azure playbook
# Cloud Shell has Ansible with azure.azcollection pre-installed
ansible --version
ansible-galaxy collection list | grep azure

# Example: list Azure VMs using Ansible dynamic inventory
cat > list_vms.yml << 'EOF'
- hosts: localhost
  connection: local
  tasks:
    - name: List all Azure VMs
      azure.azcollection.azure_rm_virtualmachine_info:
        resource_group: myRG
      register: vms
    - name: Print VM names
      debug:
        msg: "{{ item.name }} ({{ item.location }})"
      loop: "{{ vms.vms }}"
EOF

ansible-playbook list_vms.yml
A rich set of text processing tools is available for parsing JSON/YAML output from Azure CLI commands, building pipelines, and processing data.
Parse and filter az CLI JSON output
Monitor resource status every N seconds
Call REST APIs directly, test webhook endpoints
Test --query expressions before using them in az

# jq: extract resource names from az output
az resource list --resource-group myRG -o json | \
  jq -r '.[] | select(.type == "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines") | .name'

# jq: compute total count by resource type
az resource list --resource-group myRG -o json | \
  jq 'group_by(.type) | map({type: .[0].type, count: length}) | sort_by(.count) | reverse'

# yq: update a Kubernetes deployment image tag
yq e '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image = "myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:v2.1"' \
  deployment.yaml > deployment-updated.yaml

# watch: monitor an AKS node pool scaling operation
watch -n 5 'az aks nodepool show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myAKS \
  --name nodepool1 \
  --query "{state:provisioningState, count:count}" -o table'

# curl: call Azure REST API directly
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
SUB=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups?api-version=2021-04-01" | \
  jq '.value[].name'
Git and related tools are pre-installed, and your ~/.ssh and ~/.gitconfig files are persisted in ~/clouddrive. This means SSH keys and Git identities survive session restarts.

# Configure Git identity (persisted in ~/clouddrive/.gitconfig)
git config --global user.name 'Your Name'
git config --global user.email 'you@example.com'

# Generate an SSH key (saved to ~/clouddrive/.ssh)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C 'cloudshell@azure' -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 -N ''
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub   # add to GitHub/Azure DevOps SSH keys

# Clone a repository to clouddrive (persists)
cd ~/clouddrive
git clone git@github.com:myorg/myrepo.git
cd myrepo

# Authenticate with Azure DevOps using token
git config --global credential.helper store
# Then git clone https://dev.azure.com/... and enter PAT when prompted

# Git operations work exactly as in a local terminal
git log --oneline -10
git diff HEAD~1
git push origin main
For editing files directly in Cloud Shell without the VS Code tunnel, several text editors are pre-installed.
Full graphical editor in browser, ideal for most users

# Open a file in the Cloud Shell built-in Monaco editor
code ~/clouddrive/deploy.sh
# This opens the file in the editor panel on the right side of Cloud Shell

# Vim: quick edits
vim ~/.bashrc
# i to insert, Esc then :wq to save and quit

# Nano: beginner-friendly editing
nano ~/clouddrive/config.json
# Ctrl+O to save, Ctrl+X to exit

# Create and edit a file with nano from the command line
nano << 'EOF' > ~/clouddrive/notes.txt
Session notes
EOF

Tool versions in Cloud Shell are updated by the Azure team on a regular cadence. To see what is currently installed and check for version-sensitive compatibility issues, run version checks at the start of a session.

# Print versions of the most important tools
echo '=== Azure Management ==='
az version --output json | jq -r '."azure-cli"'
bicep --version
azcopy --version 2>&1 | head -1

echo '=== Kubernetes ==='
kubectl version --client --short 2>/dev/null || kubectl version --client
helm version --short

echo '=== Languages ==='
python3 --version
node --version
go version
dotnet --version

echo '=== IaC ==='
terraform --version | head -1
ansible --version | head -1

echo '=== Text tools ==='
jq --version
yq --version 2>&1 | head -1
git --version

# PowerShell: check versions
Write-Host '=== Az Module ===' -ForegroundColor Cyan
Get-Module Az -ListAvailable | Select-Object -First 1 | Select-Object Name, Version

Write-Host '=== Kubernetes ===' -ForegroundColor Cyan
kubectl version --client
helm version --short

Write-Host '=== Languages ===' -ForegroundColor Cyan
@('python3', 'node', 'go', 'dotnet') | ForEach-Object {
    $v = & $_ --version 2>&1
    Write-Host "$_: $v"
}

Tool versions in Cloud Shell are managed by Microsoft and cannot be downgraded or pinned. If your scripts require a specific version of a tool, document the version used and test on the Cloud Shell version before assuming compatibility.
While Cloud Shell cannot install system packages with apt-get (no root access), you can install user-space tools to ~/clouddrive and they will persist across sessions.

# Install Python packages to user space (persists via clouddrive)
pip install --user azure-mgmt-resource azure-identity requests
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"  # add to ~/.bashrc

# Install Node.js packages globally (ephemeral — add to ~/.bashrc to re-install)
npm install -g @azure/static-web-apps-cli

# Install a Go binary to ~/clouddrive (persists)
GOPATH=~/clouddrive/go go install github.com/mikefarah/yq/v4@latest
export PATH="$HOME/clouddrive/go/bin:$PATH"

# Download a pre-compiled binary and save to clouddrive
curl -sL https://github.com/cli/cli/releases/latest/download/gh_2.43.1_linux_amd64.tar.gz | \
  tar xz -C ~/clouddrive/bin --strip-components=2 gh_2.43.1_linux_amd64/bin/gh
chmod +x ~/clouddrive/bin/gh
export PATH="$HOME/clouddrive/bin:$PATH"

# Make PATH additions persistent across sessions
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$HOME/clouddrive/bin:$HOME/clouddrive/go/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc
The real power of Cloud Shell's pre-installed toolset is in combining tools into multi-step workflows. Here are common patterns that chain Azure CLI, jq, kubectl, and scripting runtimes together.

#!/bin/bash
# Full AKS health check combining az CLI, kubectl, and jq
set -euo pipefail

RESOURCE_GROUP=myRG
CLUSTER_NAME=myAKS

echo '=== AKS Cluster Status ==='
az aks show -g $RESOURCE_GROUP -n $CLUSTER_NAME \
  --query '{state:provisioningState, k8sVersion:kubernetesVersion, nodes:agentPoolProfiles[].count}' \
  -o table

echo '=== Getting credentials ==='
az aks get-credentials -g $RESOURCE_GROUP -n $CLUSTER_NAME --overwrite-existing

echo '=== Node Status ==='
kubectl get nodes -o wide

echo '=== Pod Summary ==='
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces \
  --field-selector=status.phase!=Running \
  -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name): \(.status.phase)"'

echo '=== Resource Usage ==='
kubectl top nodes 2>/dev/null || echo 'Metrics server not installed'

echo '=== Failed Events (last 1h) ==='
kubectl get events --all-namespaces \
  --field-selector=type=Warning \
  --sort-by='.metadata.creationTimestamp' | tail -20

#!/bin/bash
# Inventory all VMs across all subscriptions you have access to
# Combines az CLI, jq, and awk for reporting

OUTPUT_FILE=~/clouddrive/vm-inventory-$(date +%Y%m%d).csv
echo 'subscription,resourceGroup,vmName,location,size,powerState' > $OUTPUT_FILE

az account list --query '[].id' -o tsv | while read SUB_ID; do
  SUB_NAME=$(az account show --subscription $SUB_ID --query name -o tsv)
  echo "Processing: $SUB_NAME"

  az vm list \
    --subscription $SUB_ID \
    --show-details \
    --query '[].{rg:resourceGroup, name:name, loc:location, size:hardwareProfile.vmSize, power:powerState}' \
    -o json 2>/dev/null | \
  jq -r --arg sub "$SUB_NAME" '.[] | [$sub, .rg, .name, .loc, .size, .power] | @csv' >> $OUTPUT_FILE
done

echo "Inventory saved to: $OUTPUT_FILE"
wc -l $OUTPUT_FILE
Deploy and Monitor with Python SDK

# Install Azure SDK packages (user-space, persists)
pip install --user azure-mgmt-resource azure-mgmt-compute azure-identity --quiet

python3 << 'PYEOF'
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient
import subprocess, json

# Get subscription ID from CLI
sub_id = subprocess.check_output(
    ['az', 'account', 'show', '--query', 'id', '-o', 'tsv']
).decode().strip()

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
compute = ComputeManagementClient(credential, sub_id)

# List VMs and their power states
print(f'{'VM Name':<30} {'RG':<20} {'Status'}')
print('-' * 70)
for vm in compute.virtual_machines.list_all():
    iv = compute.virtual_machines.instance_view(vm.id.split('/')[4], vm.name)
    state = next((s.display_status for s in iv.statuses if 'PowerState' in s.code), 'Unknown')
    print(f'{vm.name:<30} {vm.id.split("/")[4]:<20} {state}')
PYEOF
Cloud Shell includes standard network diagnostic tools useful for testing connectivity, inspecting TLS, and debugging Azure networking configurations.
Test API endpoints, webhook URLs, health checks
Verify Private DNS zones, custom domain resolution
Verify port connectivity to VMs, databases
Inspect expiry, CN, SANs of a certificate
Test basic connectivity to VMs (if ICMP allowed)
Connect to Linux VMs, jump hosts, Bastion tunnels

# Test an Azure App Service endpoint
curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code} %{time_total}s\n' \
  https://myapp.azurewebsites.net/health

# Verify DNS resolution for a Private Endpoint
nslookup mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net
# Should return a 10.x.x.x private IP if Private DNS Zone is configured correctly

# Check if port 5432 (PostgreSQL) is reachable from Cloud Shell
nc -zv mypostgres.postgres.database.azure.com 5432

# Inspect a TLS certificate expiry
echo | openssl s_client -connect myapp.azurewebsites.net:443 2>/dev/null | \
  openssl x509 -noout -dates

# SSH to an Azure Linux VM via Azure Bastion tunnel
az network bastion ssh \
  --name myBastion \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --target-resource-id $(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query id -o tsv) \
  --auth-type ssh-key \
  --username azureuser \
  --ssh-key ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
Cloud Shell Tools Compared to Local Installations
Understanding where Cloud Shell's pre-installed toolset differs from a typical local developer machine helps set correct expectations.
Pinnable; explicit update with Update-Module Az
Recent stable; may lag cluster API version by one minor
Pinnable to match cluster version exactly
Recent stable; cannot pin older versions
Full version management via tfenv or asdf
Full docker build and compose support
Auto via portal credentials; no login needed
Requires az login or service principal setup
Outbound internet; no inbound; no private IP by default
Using Cloud Shell Tools in Scripts and Automation
Pre-installed tools are designed to work together in scripts. Here are patterns for writing robust scripts that leverage the full Cloud Shell toolset.

#!/bin/bash
# Robust script template: uses multiple Cloud Shell pre-installed tools
set -euo pipefail

# --- Configuration ---
ENVIRONMENT=${1:-dev}
RESOURCE_GROUP="myapp-${ENVIRONMENT}"
AKS_CLUSTER="myaks-${ENVIRONMENT}"

# --- Helper functions ---
log() { echo "[$(date '+%H:%M:%S')] $*"; }
require() { command -v "$1" &>/dev/null || { log "ERROR: $1 not found"; exit 1; }; }

# Check required tools
require az
require kubectl
require helm
require jq

log "Deploying to: $ENVIRONMENT"

# --- Deploy infrastructure ---
log 'Creating/updating resource group'
az group create --name $RESOURCE_GROUP --location eastus -o none

log 'Deploying Bicep template'
DEPLOYMENT=$(az deployment group create \
  --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP \
  --template-file ./infra/main.bicep \
  --parameters environment=$ENVIRONMENT \
  --query 'properties.outputs' -o json)

AKS_NAME=$(echo $DEPLOYMENT | jq -r '.aksName.value')
log "AKS cluster: $AKS_NAME"

# --- Configure Kubernetes access ---
az aks get-credentials -g $RESOURCE_GROUP -n $AKS_NAME --overwrite-existing

# --- Deploy application ---
log 'Deploying Helm chart'
helm upgrade --install myapp ./charts/myapp \
  --namespace myapp \
  --create-namespace \
  --set image.tag=${IMAGE_TAG:-latest} \
  --wait --timeout 5m

log 'Deployment complete'
kubectl get pods -n myapp

Azure Cloud Shell provides a fully equipped cloud-based terminal with dozens of pre-installed tools covering Azure management (az CLI, Az PowerShell, Bicep, AzCopy), Kubernetes (kubectl, helm), infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Ansible), development languages (Python 3, Node.js, Go, .NET), text processing (jq, yq, awk), source control (git), and editors (Monaco, vim, nano). All tools are pre-authenticated and immediately usable without local installation or configuration. Tools that are not pre-installed can be added to ~/clouddrive as user-space binaries or packages and will persist between sessions.
